content:
Systematic **chart-type guidance** for data analysis matured alongside **exploratory data analysis (EDA)** as a discipline. John Tukey's work in the 1970s emphasized letting data suggest hypotheses through visual summaries—histograms, box plots, and scatter matrices—before formal modeling.

William Cleveland and Robert McGill's **graphical perception** research (1980s) ranked chart encodings by how accurately humans decode them—position along a common scale (bar, scatter) generally beats angle (pie) and area. That research underpins modern advice to prefer **bar over pie** and **line only for ordered data**.

In ML workflows, the **grammar of graphics** ideas (Wilkinson, later **ggplot2** and **Seaborn**) separated **what** to show (distribution, relationship, comparison) from **how** to draw it. Python's stack crystallized: **Matplotlib** as renderer, **Seaborn** and **pandas** for statistical defaults, **Plotly** for interactivity—while the **question-to-chart decision** remained a cross-library skill taught in courses and EDA checklists.

Today, automated ML and LLM-assisted notebooks make it easier than ever to produce charts—but also easier to emit the **wrong** chart with confident styling. This topic preserves the pre-library decision step that Tukey-style EDA and graphical-perception research established.

timeline.label: 1970s — Tukey EDA
timeline.description: Exploratory data analysis popularized histograms, box plots, and scatter as hypothesis-generating tools before confirmatory statistics.

timeline.label: 1984 — Graphical perception (Cleveland & McGill)
timeline.description: Empirical ranking of visual encodings informed guidelines favoring bar and position-based charts over pie and area tricks.

timeline.label: 1999 — Grammar of graphics (Wilkinson)
timeline.description: Formalized mapping from data variables to aesthetic roles—foundation for ggplot2, Seaborn, and Vega-Lite thinking.

timeline.label: 2010s — Python ML visualization stack
timeline.description: Matplotlib, Seaborn, and Plotly layered implementation; chart-type literacy became a prerequisite skill separate from any one API.
